LA City LA County Guides Launch
Routing guide LADBS vs EPIC-LA

Los Angeles City vs County Permits

In Los Angeles, “the wrong permit office” is not a minor mistake. It can change search results, timeline assumptions, and even whether the address shows up in the system you are using. This guide is built for the common case: you have a property, a project, or a permit number, and you need to route it correctly before the file goes sideways.

Request Permit History + Risk Report Open LA County page
1. Why the distinction matters

City and county records do not behave like one shared permit stack.

People often use “LA” to mean the broader region, but permit routing still depends on the actual jurisdiction serving the property. If you start in the wrong system, the record can look incomplete when the real issue is simply that the address belongs somewhere else.

  • Los Angeles city coverage is the right lane for city-served LADBS addresses.
  • Los Angeles County is the right lane for county-served or unincorporated areas handled through EPIC-LA.
  • Some teams only discover the routing problem after quoting work or entering escrow. That is later than you want.
Quick rule

Do not assume the mailing identity answers the permit question.

A Los Angeles mailing address does not automatically mean a City of Los Angeles permit lane. Treat jurisdiction as something to verify, not a label to inherit from a listing, text thread, or old set of notes.

2. Fast routing workflow

Use a short sequence before you open multiple portals.

Start with the exact address

Clean up the street number, street name, unit, and ZIP so you are not chasing avoidable search issues in two systems at once.

Check the city lane first if the property is believed to be inside Los Angeles

If nothing sensible appears, do not force the conclusion. Move to the county lane and compare.

Keep the project context nearby

Type of work, rough timing, and whether the file is for buying, bidding, or close-out will tell you how much certainty you actually need.

3. Signs the file could be county-served

Pay attention when the city story feels thin too quickly.

  • The owner or agent says “LA County” and “Los Angeles” interchangeably.
  • The property sits near an unincorporated pocket or county-served area.
  • The city portal results do not line up with the work history you would expect from the property.
  • The project appears to rely on county-facing language, documents, or prior routing notes.
4. Mixed-jurisdiction reality

Some files are not clean on first pass.

Address routing, older records, or handoffs between teams can create a file that feels mixed even when there is one controlling jurisdiction. That is usually a sign to document what you know, stop guessing, and escalate to a manual review before someone keeps moving on assumptions.

5. What to ask for

Collect the right context before you escalate.

  • Exact property address and any alternate spelling already used by the owner or agent.
  • Any known permit number, even if it may be stale or incomplete.
  • Short description of the visible or expected work.
  • Decision deadline: quote, purchase, owner signoff, lender review, or design intake.
Next step

Use a report when the routing question is slowing down the real job.

A Permit History + Risk Report is the better move when city-versus-county confusion is already affecting pricing, diligence, or internal handoff quality.

Request Permit History + Risk Report See sample report
FAQ

Common routing questions

If I have a Los Angeles ZIP code, is it always LADBS?

No. ZIP code is not a reliable substitute for permit jurisdiction.

Should I check both city and county every time?

Not always. But if the address is unclear or the first results do not make sense, checking both is often faster than forcing a bad assumption.

What is the cleanest way to hand off the issue internally?

Capture the address, known permit info, and why routing is unclear, then hand it off with a request for a Permit History + Risk Report instead of informal screenshots.
